content.problemStatement:
On the two dimensional co-ordinate plane, a mythical creature starts from the square (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) and moves to the square (N,M)(N,M)(N,M), using only down and right moves (that is, it can move from (x,y)(x, y)(x,y) to (x+1,y)(x+1, y)(x+1,y) or (x,y+1)(x, y+1)(x,y+1)).

The creature drops a single gold coin on every square on its path (including (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) and (N,M)(N, M)(N,M)). Also, it chooses any path uniformly at random, out of all possible paths from (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) to (N,M)(N,M)(N,M).

After the creature has reached its destination, Bob starts from the square (1,M)(1,M)(1,M) and moves to the square (N,1)(N,1)(N,1) using only down and left moves (that is, Bob can move from (x,y)(x, y)(x,y) to (x+1,y)(x+1, y)(x+1,y) or (x,y−1)(x, y-1)(x,y−1)).

Whenever Bob reaches a square with a gold coin, he collects it. Bob wants to **maximize** the number gold coins he collects.

Assuming Bob chooses his path optimally, what is the expected number of gold coins he will collect?

Note that Bob knows the path taken by the beast before he starts moving.

The answer can be expressed as a fraction XY\frac{X}{Y}YX​, where XXX and YYY are coprime integers and YYY is not a multiple of 109+710^9 + 7109+7.

You should compute X⋅Y−1X\cdot Y^{-1}X⋅Y−1 modulo 109+710^9+7109+7, where Y−1Y^{-1}Y−1 is the multiplicative inverse of YYY modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each of the next TTT lines contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer, modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 250250250.
The sum of MMM over all test cases won't exceed 250250250.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: There are only two paths the creature can take:

(1,1)→(1,2)→(2,2)(1,1) \to (1,2) \to (2,2)(1,1)→(1,2)→(2,2)
(1,1)→(2,1)→(2,2)(1,1) \to (2,1) \to (2,2)(1,1)→(2,1)→(2,2)

In both these paths, it can be shown that Bob can collect exactly 222 coins.
